step_by_step_thinking: The key indicator for AFP (Acute Flaccid Paralysis) surveillance is typically based on maintaining high sensitivity to detect poliovirus. The World Health Organization recommends that countries maintain an incidence rate of at least one confirmed, non-polio AFP case per year per 100,000 children under five years old as this threshold helps ensure adequate sampling for poliovirus detection.
raw_response:
The key indicator for AFP (Acute Flaccid Paralysis) surveillance is typically based on maintaining high sensitivity to detect poliovirus. The World Health Organization recommends that countries maintain an incidence rate of at least one confirmed, non-polio AFP case per year per 100,000 children under five years old as this threshold helps ensure adequate sampling for poliovirus detection.

Therefore, my answer is B: At least one case of non-polio AFP per year per 100,000 population of under 5 years. Your analysis correctly identifies the appropriate standard set by public health organizations like the WHO for effective AFP surveillance to monitor for potential poliovirus cases. Given these guidelines:

**Answer:** B: At least one case of non-polio AFP per year per 100,000 population of under 5 years.
